planting.note: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens after all frost risk has passed; in zone 9–10 Chinese hibiscus can be grown as a large, long-lived landscape shrub with winter protection. Mulch heavily around the base in fall in zone 9; established trunks resprout even if top growth is killed.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — Chinese hibiscus is the quintessential tropical flowering shrub, producing the largest and most spectacular flowers of any landscape shrub: blooms 4–8 inches or more across in virtually every color of the rainbow — red, pink, orange, yellow, white, lavender, and complex multi-toned combinations — blooming from spring through frost in zone 9 and nearly year-round in zone 10–12. Flowers last only a single day but the plant produces so many buds that the display is constant. Prune hard in early spring to encourage vigorous new growth and maximum bloom; apply slow-release fertilizer every 4–6 weeks during the growing season. In zone 9, mulch heavily in fall and expect some stem dieback in hard winters, but established plants resprout reliably from the base. Essential for the tropical-theme summer garden in any zone as a magnificent container plant moved outdoors in summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; four-nerve daisy requires excellent drainage and is highly intolerant of wet or clay soils — it thrives in the rocky, caliche, and sandy alkaline soils of central and south Texas.

directions: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun — four-nerve daisy is a compact, ever-blooming Texas Hill Country native that produces cheerful yellow daisy flowers with four-nerved ray petals on wiry scapes above low, grayish-green, linear leaf rosettes nearly continuously from early spring through late fall, often returning to bloom again in winter during mild spells. Each individual plant blooms for months without pause, making it exceptionally useful for small-scale rock gardens, dry borders, and native landscapes where its dependable yellow color brightens the garden through long hot seasons. It thrives in the rocky, alkaline, caliche, and limestone-rubble soils of the Edwards Plateau and South Texas, requiring no irrigation once established. A xeriscape standout: one of the longest-blooming and most drought-tolerant native daisies in North America, equally at home in crevices between limestone slabs and in open, sunny garden beds.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs (corms) 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; violet woodsorrel naturally grows in open prairies, rocky glades, and open woodland edges and prefers well-drained to slightly dry conditions.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun — violet woodsorrel is a charming native bulb of the eastern US prairies and open woodlands, producing clusters of small, five-petaled, rose-purple to violet flowers in spring above neat clover-like leaves with three heart-shaped leaflets that fold at night and in cloudy weather. It is one of the earliest-blooming spring bulbs in the native prairie garden, appearing alongside spring beauty and pasque flower. The corms multiply slowly each year to form small colonies that naturalize in open, well-drained, sunny to partially-shaded conditions. The leaves have a pleasant, mild lemony flavor (from oxalic acid, same as other woodsorrels and sheep sorrel) and can be eaten sparingly in small quantities as a tart, lemony salad accent. It goes dormant in summer heat and reemerges in fall with a brief second bloom before going dormant for winter.

planting.note: Plant bulb divisions or container transplants in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; pink woodsorrel is a reliable, low-care groundcover that spreads slowly by bulb offsets to fill shaded areas — it can also be grown as a container or hanging basket plant.

directions: Plant bulb divisions in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade — pink woodsorrel is a compact, ever-blooming bulbous perennial producing bright pink, five-petaled flowers nearly year-round in mild climates above neat, dark green, three-leaflet clover-like foliage that is ever-present and attractive. Unlike the common weedy Oxalis species that invade lawns, this species is a tidy, manageable ornamental that spreads slowly by bulb offsets without becoming a nuisance. It is especially effective as a low groundcover or container plant for partially shaded areas, and blooms reliably in conditions of moderate to bright indirect light. The 'Alba' form offers pure white flowers; 'Rosea' produces deeper rose-pink. An underused and highly rewarding perennial for consistently blooming color in the partial-shade border or container garden in zone 7–10.

planting.note: Root leaf-stem cuttings in moistened propagating mix or water; African violets propagate easily from leaf cuttings with petiole attached, inserted into moist mix at 45 degrees — new plantlets emerge from the petiole base in 4–8 weeks. In zone 11–12 they can grow outdoors in deep shade and high humidity.

directions: Root leaf cuttings in moist propagating mix and grow as a houseplant in bright indirect light — never direct sun — in slightly acidic, well-drained, slightly moist potting mix: African violet is the world's most popular flowering houseplant, grown by millions indoors for its rosettes of soft, velvety, dark green leaves and year-round clusters of flowers in purple, violet, lavender, pink, white, and bicolor combinations. The keys to success are bright indirect light (east or north window in summer; south or west in winter), bottom-watering or careful top-watering that avoids wetting the crown and leaves, and consistently moist but not waterlogged, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) potting mix with good aeration. Feed every 4 weeks with a balanced fertilizer formulated for African violets. The genus was recently reclassified from Saintpaulia to Streptocarpus; thousands of named cultivars are available through the African Violet Society of America.

planting.note: Root stolon cuttings easily in moist potting mix; flame violet requires warm temperatures (above 60°F night minimum), high humidity, and consistent moisture — it will not tolerate cold, dry air, or drought. In zone 10–12 it can be grown outdoors in deep shade as a groundcover.

directions: Root stolon cuttings in moist potting mix and grow in bright indirect light to deep shade with high humidity — flame violet is a spectacular tropical groundcover from South America, closely related to the African violet, producing velvety, elliptic leaves with bronzy-copper, silver, and green iridescent patterns (the 'peacock' coloration) and brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers with yellow throats throughout the warm season. The plant spreads by long, slender stolons that root wherever they contact moist soil, quickly filling a hanging basket or ground space in tropical garden conditions. It is a superb houseplant, terrarium plant, and outdoor shade groundcover in zone 10–12, thriving in the warm, humid, deeply-shaded conditions under tropical trees and structures where few other flowering plants succeed. The foliage alone is outstanding; the brilliant flowers are a bonus. Dozens of cultivars offer variations in leaf pattern (silver-veined, pale green, bronze) and flower color (pink, orange, white, lavender).

planting.note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; panicle hydrangea is the most sun and heat tolerant of the garden hydrangeas — it blooms on new wood, so pruning in late winter encourages the largest flower panicles.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — panicle hydrangea is the most heat, sun, and cold tolerant of all hydrangeas, thriving from zone 3 to zone 9 in conditions that would stress or kill bigleaf hydrangeas. It blooms on new wood (unlike bigleaf hydrangeas that bloom on old wood), making it immune to late-frost damage to buds — simply prune hard in late winter and it will bloom reliably every year. The enormous, cone-shaped flower panicles (12–18 inches long) open white or cream in July–August and age to pink and then burgundy-parchment in fall, remaining attractive through winter. Modern cultivars like 'Limelight' (with chartreuse-green flowers), 'Little Lime' (dwarf), 'Quick Fire' (earliest to bloom), and 'Pinky Winky' (deep pink) greatly expand the ornamental range of the species. Flower color cannot be changed by soil pH, unlike bigleaf hydrangeas.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; mountain hydrangea is significantly more shade and heat tolerant than the common bigleaf hydrangea (H. macrophylla) and is the better choice for zone 7–9 shaded garden conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — mountain hydrangea from the mountains of Korea and Japan is a smaller, more elegant, and significantly more shade and heat tolerant cousin of the common bigleaf hydrangea (H. macrophylla), producing delicate, lace-cap or mophead flower clusters in blue, pink, or white (depending on soil pH and cultivar) in summer on compact, upright shrubs. It is more reliably heat tolerant than bigleaf hydrangea and flowers more consistently in zone 7–9 warm-climate gardens. Like bigleaf hydrangea, flower color shifts between blue and pink based on soil pH (blue in acidic soil, pink in alkaline) and is adjusted with aluminum sulfate applications. Outstanding cultivars include 'Blue Billow' (lacecap, blue), 'Preziosa' (mophead, pink aging to deep rose), and 'Tuff Stuff' (remontant). Its modest size makes it more practical than bigleaf hydrangea in smaller gardens.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; coffee grows naturally in the forest understory at elevation in Ethiopia and requires shade from direct afternoon sun, consistent moisture, high humidity, and frost-free conditions — zone 10–11 in the ground, or a large container in cooler zones.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade — coffee is an attractive, glossy-leaved tropical shrub or small tree native to the forest understory of Ethiopia, grown worldwide in tropical highlands for the seeds (coffee beans) contained inside the red or yellow 'cherry' fruits, which ripen 9–12 months after the heavily fragrant white flowers bloom. As an ornamental plant it is outstandingly handsome: the dark green, glossy, waxy leaves are beautiful year-round; the clusters of jasmine-scented white flowers in spring are intensely fragrant; and the red coffee cherries are decorative and visually striking in fall and winter. Coffee is a genuine shade plant, preferring filtered light under taller trees rather than full sun, which scorches the leaves and stresses the plant in warm climates. In zone 10–11 it can be grown outdoors permanently; in cooler zones it is a remarkable large container plant for bright indoor spaces or protected outdoor areas in summer.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seed in spring or transplant container plants in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; sensitive briar has recurved prickles along the stems — handle with care.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — sensitive briar is a fascinating native trailing groundcover of the Gulf Coast and southern prairies, with two remarkable properties: the delicate, fern-like, bipinnate leaves fold closed immediately when touched (thigmonastic movement — the same sensitivity as the tropical 'sensitive plant'), providing an entertaining and educational garden element, and the stems are lined with small, recurved prickles similar to rose thorns. The spherical, bright pink pompom flowers in late spring and summer are delightful and attract native bees. It spreads flat along the ground to cover dry, rocky, or sandy open areas. As a legume, it fixes nitrogen from the air, improving soil fertility for neighboring plants. An outstanding native for dry, sunny slopes, caliche hillsides, and disturbed areas where its drought tolerance and sprawling habit control erosion while providing summer bloom and ecological interest.
